content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

September 8, 1983


Charles Wachob
City Attorney
1103 High Street
Auburn, CA  95603

Re:  Advice Letter No. A-83-196

Dear Mr. Wachob:

     Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Auburn
City Councilmember Gene Wise.  The facts, as stated in your
letter are that Mr. Wise resides in the vicinity of Huntley
Avenue in Auburn.  Since 1980, the residents of this area have
advocated rezoning the area to lower the density of living units
which can be constructed on the lots in the area.  Prior to this
becoming a City Councilmember, Mr. Wise spoke in support of this
before both the Planning Commission and the City Council.

     In 1983, after Mr. Wise was elected to the Council, a
request for a rezoning was once again introduced.  The current
proposal requests that the area be rezoned from its current R-3
designation (which allows 10-15 living units per acre) to R-1-7
(which allows one single family living unit per 7,000 square
feet).  The effect of this change will be to ban the
construction of apartments and duplexes from the area, which the
residents hope will help to maintain the present older,
residential character of the neighborhood and encourage the
restoration of Victorian-style homes and other older homes.

     On August 3, 1983, the Planning Commission approved the
requested general plan amendment for the Huntley area and
recommended to the Council that the rezoning from R-3 to R-1-7
be approved.

     In setting the boundaries for the rezoning, the Planning
Commission formally decided to exclude several parcels that the
residents wanted included in the rezone area.  One of the
excluded parcels is located near to Mr. Wise's residence.  In

Charles Wachob
September 8, 1983
Page 2


1980, the owner, Mr. Sherman, received approval to split his lot
into three parcels.  Recently, he received permits to construct
duplexes on each of these parcels.  Because the permits have
been issued, he will be able to proceed with this construction,
regardless of whether or not his property is included in the
rezone area.  The other excluded lots are on a different street
and are several blocks away from Mr. Wise's property.

The Huntley Avenue residents have filed an appeal to the
City Council with respect to the determination to exclude the
lots.  The Council will consider this appeal, the general plan
amendment request, some related environmental determinations and
the rezone request at the same meeting.  Your question is
whether the provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent
Mr. Wise from participating in these decisions.

DISCUSSION

Government Code Section 87100 1/ prohibits a public
official from making or participating in the making of a decision
if he knows or has reason to know that he has a financial
interest.

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

* * *

(b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

Section 87103.

Mr. Wise's home in the Huntley Avenue area is valued at over
$1,000.  He may not make or participate in the decision to
approve or disapprove the rezoning if it is reasonably

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Charles Wachob
September 8, 1983
Page 3


foreseeable that the decision will increase or decrease the
fair market value of the property by the lesser of:

     1.  Ten thousand dollars ($10,000); or

     2.  One half of one percent if the effect is one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                         (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                         18702(b)(2)(B)1. and 2.)

If it is foreseeable that the decision to approve or disapprove
the rezoning will have a material financial effect on the
property, Mr. Wise also may not make or participate in the
decisions concerning the appeal on the boundaries issue, the
general plan amendment, or the environmental concerns, to the
extent that decisions on these may affect whether the rezoning
is approved or disapproved.

     In a telephone conversation, you advised me that you asked
the Placer County Assessor to evaluate the impact that the
proposed rezoning would have on Mr. Wise's property. He
informed you that he did not feel that there would be any
increase or decrease in the fair market value. Unless Mr. Wise
has a reason to believe that the assessor was wrong in his
determination regarding the impact of downzoning on his
house, or on the neighborhood surrounding his house, he can
participate in the decisions.2/

     As to the decision on whether to include or exclude various
parcels in the rezone area, Mr. Wise must disqualify himself
from this decision if it will foreseeably have a material
financial effect on his real property interest. You stated that
the parcel closest to Mr. Wise's property will have duplexes
built on it regardless of the zoning, and the other parcels in
question are several blocks away. Thus, it appears unlikely

___________________________

     2/ If Mr. Wise determines that the approval or
disapproval will foreseeably have a material financial effect,
the "public generally" exception does not apply (Section
87103). The decision will affect approximately 3% of the single
family units and 2% of the total single and multi-family units
in Auburn. This does not constitute a "significant segment of
the public" as is required by 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703,
copy enclosed.

Charles Wachob
September 8, 1983
Page 4


that this decision will have a material financial affect on his
property.

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:plh

CITY OF AUBURN
1103 High Street
Auburn, California 95603

(916) 885-5661

August 24, 1983


Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Ms. McLean:

This letter is to follow-up on our previous telephone conversation concerning a possible conflict of interest issue which may arise with respect to City Councilman Gene Wise of the City of Auburn. Specifically, I seek your advice and opinion as to whether it would be proper for Councilman Wise to participate on upcoming City Council action concerning a proposed rezone of an Auburn residential area within which Councilman Wise resides. In order that we can proceed within the confines of all relevant conflict of interest guidelines, and in order that you may fully appreciate the factual circumstances of this potential issue, a brief review of the history of the underlying rezone issue is useful and, accordingly, is set forth in some detail below.

In 1980, residents of an Auburn residential area located in the general vicinity of Huntley Avenue first began to request that their neighborhood undergo a formal rezone. At that time it was being requested that there be a general plan amendment from a high residential category (10 to 15 living units per acre) to a low density residential category (2 to 4 units per acre) concerning the Huntley Avenue area. The precise zoning change requested at that time was from R-3 to R1-10, as set forth above.

In accordance with CEQA a limited environmental impact report was prepared for the City of Auburn concerning the Huntley area rezone. On April 7, 1981 Mr. Wise spoke in favor of the proposed down zone at a hearing before the Auburn Planning Commission. After public hearing, the Planning Commission at that time denied both the general plan amendment and the rezone request. Finally, at a regular meeting of the City Council on March 23, 1981 the City Council considered the proposed general plan amendment and rezone for the Huntley area. The official City Council minutes for that

Janis Shank McLean
Page Two
August 24, 1983


meeting indicate that Mr. Wise spoke to rezone issues in his
capacity as an interested resident of the proposed rezone area.
The City Council denied the rezone request and general plan
amendment proposed at that time.

Keeping to this chronological review, it should be noted that
Mr. Wise was then elected to the Auburn City Council in March,
1982.

Next, a slightly modified version of the Huntley Avenue rezone
request appeared in 1983. As during the period of the previous
rezone request, Mr. Wise continues to reside in the Huntley area.
This time, however, the residents of the Huntley area are
requesting that their area be rezoned from its remaining R-3
designation to R-1-7 (single family, 7,000 sq. ft.).

To date, the Planning Commission has formally reconsidered the
sufficiency of the original limited EIR concerning the Huntley
area. Additionally, the Planning Commission on August 3, 1983
approved the requested general plan amendment for the Huntley
area and recommended to the Council that the Huntley area be
rezoned from R-3 to R-1-7. In this connection, the Planning
Commission made formal findings centering on the maintenance of
the present older residential character of the neighborhood. It
is a recognized intent of the rezone proponents to maintain the
character of the neighborhood and to encourage the preservation
of Victorian style and other older homes in the Huntley area.

The Planning Commission in its determination of the boundaries
of the rezone area, formally decided to exclude several parcels
which residents wanted to have included in the rezone area.
While this in itself is not unusual it is mentioned for the reason
that one of the excluded parcels is located near Mr. Wise's own
home. The other excluded parcels are on the opposite end of the pro-
posed rezone area from the Wise residence. A map is attached
hereto for the purpose of illustrating these locations.

It should be noted that Huntley area residents have filed an ap-
peal to the City Council with respect to the Planning Commission
determination to exclude these several parcels from the rezone
area. Presently, that appeal is set to be heard by the City Council
on September 12, 1983. The Council will also consider at that time
some related environmental determinations, the general plan amend-
ment request and the rezone request itself.

Janis Shank McLean
Page Three
August 24, 1983


while it may be completely irrelevant to your determination of
conflict of interest issues, you should know that the "excluded
lot" closest to Mr. Wise's own residence is owned by a Mr.
Melvin Sherman.   In June, 1980 the Planning Commission approved
a negative declaration for a lot split of Mr. Sherman's property
in this area.  Accordingly, the lot was split into three parcels
and Mr. Sherman proposed that a duplex be built on each of the
three parcels.  Having long ago been dealt with by the Planning
Commission, Mr. Sherman this year applied for building permits
from the City to construct duplexes on the three parcels.  Per-
mits have not been approved but it is conceivable that they will
be either before the City Council takes formal action on Sept-
ember 12, 1983 or before final hearings are held on the general
plan amendment.  At present, he is in the "plan check" process
with the Building Department awaiting official review of energy
calculations for the construction of the duplexes.  It is safe
to say that the fact that these duplexes may be built at all is
of high concern to many of the residents of the Huntley area
who have proposed a downzone for these many years.  Upon comple-
tion of the "plan check" the City could have a ministerial duty
to issue the permits and the ability of Mr. Sherman to construct
his duplexes on these three parcels and to avoid the effect of
downzone may well be regarded as a moot issue.

As a further consideration it should noted that approximately 75
living units are in the proposed Huntley rezone area.  As of 1982
there were approximately 1,190 multi-family units within the City
and 2,470 single family units.

Last, for the purposes of your determination, you may assume that
Mr. Wise has stated no intent to sell his home in the Huntley
area notwithstanding any action that may be taken with respect to
the rezone request.  You should also know that Mr. Wise is, to my
knowledge, not a licensed real estate broker or agent who may be-
come involved with real property sales.  You may also assume that
this also applies to members of his immediate family who reside
with him.  In fact, Mr. Wise is an employee of the telephone
company and works in Sacramento.

You may also wish to know that the City of Auburn has by formal
resolution (Resolution 80-83, June 9, 1980) adopted a model
conflict of interest code.

Janis Shank McLean
Page Four
August 24, 1983


Given the fact that Mr. Wise resides in the proposed rezone
area it is not unforeseeable that someone may raise this issue.
We wish to proceed with all due caution. I have previously
discussed this matter with a staff attorney at the League of
California Cities and it was our preliminary shared opinion
that there is no conflict of interest present which would
require disqualification of Mr. Wise. It is my own general
perception that it is not reasonably foreseeable that any
decision that Mr. Wise may make on the upcoming City Council
issues could have any material financial effect on him dis-
tinguishable from a general public effect. It occurs to me
that were we to accept the argument that an elected City
Councilman had to disqualify himself from participating in
a rezone area for the mere reason that he resided in the area
there would be countless occasions where councilmen would have
to disqualify themselves on issues of general public importance
where there was no discernible or foreseeable probability of
personal financial gain or loss. Notwithstanding, however,
we would appreciate your professional opinion as to this issue,
preferably in written form.

Thank you for letting me bring this to your attention and we
look forward to your response. If you need any further ad-
ditional information concerning this, of course, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

Yours very truly,

Charles Wachob
City Attorney

sections.conclusion: Unless Mr. Wise has a reason to believe that the assessor was wrong in his determination regarding the impact of the downzoning on his house, or on the neighborhood surrounding his house, he can participate in the decisions. As to the decision on whether to include or exclude various parcels in the rezone area, Mr. Wise must disqualify himself from this decision if it will foreseeably have a material financial effect on his real property interest. Thus, it appears unlikely that this decision will have a material financial affect on his property.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a public 
official from making or participating the making of a decision 
if he knows or has reason to know that he has a financial 
interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on:
sections.question_synthetic: Can City Councilmember Gene Wise participate in city council decisions regarding a proposed rezoning of the Huntley Avenue area where he resides, given potential conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Wise can participate in the rezoning decision if the Placer County Assessor's determination that the rezoning will not materially affect his property value is reliable. He must disqualify himself from boundary decisions only if they would foreseeably have a material financial effect on his property; given that the closest excluded parcel will be developed regardless of zoning and other parcels are several blocks away, such an effect appears unlikely.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated August 24, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 8, 1983). Analysis focuses on the FPPC response. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly impact comprehension. Statutory references to Government Code Section 87100 and 87103 and administrative code sections included.

in Auburn.

Charles Wachob 
September 8, 1983  
that this decision will have a material financial affect on his 
property.

CONCLUSION: Unless Mr. Wise has a reason to believe that the assessor was wrong in his determination regarding the impact of the downzoning on his house, or on the neighborhood surrounding his house, he can participate in the decisions. As to the decision on whether to include or exclude various parcels in the rezone area, Mr. Wise must disqualify himself from this decision if it will foreseeably have a material financial effect on his real property interest. Thus, it appears unlikely that this decision will have a material financial affect on his property.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether City Councilmember Gene Wise can participate in decisions regarding a proposed rezoning of the Huntley Avenue residential area where he resides. The letter concludes that absent evidence suggesting the assessor was incorrect, Wise can participate in the main rezoning decision, and likely can participate in boundary decisions as well since the rezoning is unlikely to have a material financial effect on his property.
